planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural cold stratification or plant container-grown specimens in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; one-seed juniper is among the most drought-tolerant and cold-hardy junipers of the Southwest, growing naturally on dry mesas and rocky slopes from 3,500–7,000 feet elevation.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; one-seed juniper is the most abundant and widespread tree of the Colorado Plateau and central New Mexico highlands, forming the characteristic juniper-piñon woodland that covers millions of acres of the Southwest at mid-elevations. The name comes from the berry-like cones, each containing only one seed (rather than the 2–3 of most junipers). A characteristically multi-trunked, dense, irregularly shaped tree with scaly, gray-green foliage and shredding reddish-brown bark. Often grows as a twisted, gnarled form in exposed, rocky, windswept sites. Extremely drought-tolerant and long-lived — ancient trees 300–500 years old are common. The 'berries' are important food for many birds including bluebirds and robins. The wood is used for fence posts and fuel; the shredded bark was used by Indigenous peoples for basket-making and insulation. An excellent low-maintenance xeriscape tree for hot, dry, rocky sites in the Southwest.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural cold stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to arid, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; Utah juniper is supremely adapted to the cold desert climate and thin soils of the Great Basin and Colorado Plateau at 3,000–8,000 feet elevation.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to arid, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; Utah juniper is the defining tree of the Great Basin and Colorado Plateau desert — the dominant woody plant of the vast juniper-sagebrush and piñon-juniper woodlands that characterize the landscape of Nevada, Utah, western Colorado, and adjacent states at mid-elevations. More adapted to the cold desert continental climate (extreme heat, extreme cold, minimal rainfall) than almost any other tree species. Often grows in nearly pure stands on rocky mesas, canyon slopes, and valley floors. The species name 'osteosperma' means 'bony-seeded' — the hard seed within the fleshy berry-like cone. Important forage and browse plant for deer, pronghorn, and other wildlife. The berries are a critical food source for many birds. An excellent xeriscape tree for high desert gardens (zone 4–8) where water conservation is paramount — virtually no irrigation needed after establishment.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral or slightly acidic, dry to average, rocky or loamy soil in full sun; Rocky Mountain juniper tolerates a wide range of soil types and is very drought tolerant once established. Use container-grown plants for best success — bare-root establishment can be difficult.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral or slightly acidic, dry to average, rocky or loamy soil in full sun; Rocky Mountain juniper (also called Colorado red cedar) is the western counterpart of eastern red cedar — the dominant juniper of the Rocky Mountains and Great Plains, growing from British Columbia south through the Rockies and east across the northern Great Plains to North Dakota and Kansas. Valuable wildlife tree — the blue-glaucous berry-like cones are an important food source for cedar waxwings, Townsend's solitaires, and many other birds. Widely used for windbreaks and shelterbelts on the Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills. The cultivar 'Skyrocket' (also sold as J. virginiana 'Skyrocket') is one of the most narrowly columnar ornamental conifers available — forming a tight, fastigiate column only 2–3 feet wide and 15–20 feet tall, ideal for narrow spaces. Blue-foliaged cultivars like 'Wichita Blue' and 'Moonglow' are popular landscape specimens. Extremely cold-hardy and drought-tolerant.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; savin juniper is one of the most widely used evergreen groundcover and bank-stabilization shrubs, with numerous cultivars including the very popular 'Tamariscifolia' (tam juniper). WARNING: All parts of savin juniper are poisonous — do not plant where children may ingest the berries.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean to average soil in full sun; savin juniper is one of the most important ornamental junipers in the landscape industry — a low, spreading, multi-stemmed shrubby juniper of mountain slopes in central Europe and western Asia that has given rise to dozens of widely grown cultivars. The cultivar 'Tamariscifolia' (tam juniper) is one of the most widely planted low-growing evergreen shrubs in North American commercial landscaping. The species and cultivars tolerate heat, cold, drought, and poor soils, making them reliable low-maintenance groundcovers for banks, slopes, and sunny exposed sites. The foliage has a distinctly pungent, somewhat unpleasant odor when crushed — quite different from the pleasant cedar scent of most junipers. All parts are poisonous if ingested; the plant contains savin oil which historically caused miscarriages and was used as an abortifacient in herbal medicine — it should never be used internally. An excellent tough groundcover for full-sun, dry, difficult slopes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; sawara cypress grows naturally in moist mountain forests of central and southern Japan and requires adequate moisture — do not plant in dry, hot, exposed conditions or in heavy alkaline clay.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, fertile to average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; sawara cypress is the most diverse of the Japanese false cypresses (Chamaecyparis) in terms of cultivated forms — dozens of named cultivars representing completely different appearances have been selected over centuries of Japanese horticulture, including the thread-like drooping forms (Filifera group), the juvenile foliage-forever plants (Squarrosa group) with soft, feathery mossy texture, the dwarf globe cultivars (Nana group), and the golden-foliaged variants of all types. The natural species is a large forest tree in Japan, but the species in gardens is usually encountered as one of its many distinctive cultivars. Requires adequate moisture and good drainage — does not tolerate alkaline soils or drought stress well. The wildly different cultivated forms (some almost unrecognizably different from the species) make this one of the most architecturally diverse of all ornamental conifers. Widely used in Japanese-influenced gardens, conifer collections, and ornamental mixed borders.
